target.name: aogacillin B
target.motivation: Aogacillins A and B are natural products capable of overcoming arbekacin resistance in methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). They possess a densely oxidized spirolactone moiety and an electron deficient terminal exocyclic double bond, posing a significant challenge for total synthesis. The compounds also show potential for treating renal insufficiency, renal carcinoma, breast cancer, Alzheimer's disease, and type-2 diabetes.

success_criteria.free_text: Achieve the first and asymmetric total synthesis of aogacillin B and ent-aogacillin A.

key_disconnections.reasoning: The d-lactone could be constructed by aldol condensation of a lactic acid derivative and a β-hydroxyl-α-methylene-aldehyde followed by lactonization.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond between the cyclohexanone and the dihydropyranone
key_disconnections.reasoning: The spirocyclic skeleton could be efficiently constructed by the Achmatowicz rearrangement of a furfuryl alcohol derivative.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond between the cyclohexanone and the furan
key_disconnections.reasoning: Addition of 2-lithiofuran to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone delivers the substrate for the Achmatowicz rearrangement.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the exocyclic alkene
key_disconnections.reasoning: To avoid issues with olefination at a later stage, the terminal alkene was proposed to be installed at an early stage through vinyl metallic reagent addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone, followed by allylic oxidation.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The initial strategy relied on the Achmatowicz rearrangement to build the spirocyclic framework, followed by functionalization of the dihydropyranone to the lactone and installation of the exocyclic alkene. This strategy failed due to difficulties in olefination of a sterically hindered ketone. A revised strategy was developed, focusing on early installation of the terminal alkene via Grignard addition and allylic oxidation, followed by a one-pot aldol reaction and lactonization to form the d-lactone.

thought.background: Aogacillins A and B are potent antibiotics with a challenging structure featuring a densely oxidized spirolactone and an electron-deficient exocyclic double bond.
thought.gap: How to efficiently construct the complex spirocyclic d-lactone core and introduce the exocyclic double bond.
thought.decision: To construct the spirocyclic skeleton, the Achmatowicz rearrangement was chosen as a key strategic transformation, starting from furfuryl alcohol II. The d-lactone would be generated from the dihydropyranone acetal moiety of intermediate I. Intermediate II would be derived from the addition of 2-lithiofuran to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (III).

evidence.text_span: Our original retrosynthetic analysis was proposed in 2013 and hinged on the Achmatowicz rearrangement as depicted in Scheme 1. The fully functionalized d-lactone of aogacillins A and B could be generated from the dihydropyranone acetal moiety of intermediate I, which was derived from the Achmatowicz rearrangement of furfuryl alcohol II. The addition of 2- lithiofuran to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone delivered II as the substrate for the Achmatowicz rearrangement.

thought.background: The synthesis requires cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (5) as a starting material.
thought.gap: How to synthesize cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (5) in a scalable manner.
thought.decision: To prepare 5, a Claisen condensation of ethyl formate with 2-methyl cyclohexanone (2) was performed, followed by enamine formation and a Benary reaction to yield E-ethylidene ketone (4). This was then hydrogenated to give the desired cis product (5).

outcome.observation: This scalable four-step-one-purification sequence provided cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5 in multigram quantities in a single batch.

evidence.text_span: As shown in Scheme 2, our synthesis commenced with the preparation of cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5. Claisen condensation of ethyl formate with commercially available 2- methyl cyclohexanone 2 (ref. 11) was followed by enamine formation and the Benary reaction12 to deliver E-ethylidene ketone 4.13 Hydrogenation with Pd/C in ethyl acetate gave the desired cis product with high yield and good diastereoselectivity. This scalable four-step-one-purification sequence provided cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5 (ref. 14) in multigram quantities in a single batch.

thought.background: With cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (5) in hand, the next step is to introduce the furan moiety and perform the Achmatowicz rearrangement.
thought.gap: How to efficiently perform the nucleophilic addition of furan and the subsequent Achmatowicz rearrangement and acetal protection.
thought.decision: Addition of lithiated furan to 5 was performed to give 6, followed by Achmatowicz rearrangement with mCPBA and protection of the labile acetal as benzyl ether to deliver 7.

outcome.observation: Addition of lithiated furan to 5 afforded the single diastereomeric compound 6 as the substrate for the Achmatowicz rearrangement, which occurred smoothly with m-chloroperoxybenzoic acid (mCPBA) to provide the spiro-dihydropyranone acetal framework. Protection of the labile acetal as benzyl ether delivered 7 with a 1:1 diastereomeric ratio at the acetal carbon. The excellent scalability of the Achmatowicz rearrangement allowed us to prepare 7 on a 30-gram scale.

evidence.text_span: Addition of lithiated furan to 5 afforded the single diastereomeric compound 6 as the substrate for the Achmatowicz rearrangement, which occurred smoothly with m-chloroperoxybenzoic acid (mCPBA) to provide the spiro-dihydropyranone acetal framework.15 Protection of the labile acetal as benzyl ether delivered 7 with a 1 : 1 diastereomeric ratio at the acetal carbon. The excellent scalability of the Achmatowicz rearrangement allowed us to prepare 7 on a 30-gram scale.

thought.background: With the spiro-framework 7, the next goal is to functionalize the dihydropyranone into the lactone corresponding to aogacillins, starting with installation of a methyl group at C3.
thought.gap: How to install the methyl group at C3 of compound 7.
thought.decision: The methyl group was installed using a methyl Grignard reagent followed by PhSeCl/H2O2 to afford compound 8.

outcome.observation: Installation of the methyl group at C3 was performed with a methyl Grignard reagent followed by PhSeCl/H2O2 to afford compound 8 in 48% yield.

evidence.text_span: Installation of the methyl group at C3 was performed with a methyl Grignard reagent followed by PhSeCl/H2O2 (ref. 16) to afford compound 8 in 48% yield.

thought.background: After installing the methyl group, the next step is to introduce another methyl group via nucleophilic 1,2-addition.
thought.gap: How to achieve efficient 1,2-addition of MeLi to compound 8.
thought.decision: CeCl3 mediated nucleophilic 1,2-addition of MeLi was attempted.

outcome.observation: CeCl3 mediated nucleophilic 1,2-addition of MeLi to 8 delivered inseparable products with poor yield. Improvement of the yield was attempted without success due to the extraordinary steric hindrance from the adjacent quaternary carbon (similar to the neopentyl effect).

evidence.text_span: CeCl3 mediated nucleophilic 1,2-addition17 of MeLi to 8 delivered inseparable products with poor yield. Improvement of the yield was attempted without success due to the extraordinary steric hindrance from the adjacent quaternary carbon (similar to the neopentyl effect).

thought.background: The direct 1,2-addition of MeLi to 8 resulted in poor yield and inseparable products.
thought.gap: How to obtain separable products after the 1,2-addition.
thought.decision: After TMS protection of the tertiary alcohol, the diastereoisomers 9a and 9b could be separated by flash column chromatography on silica gel and used individually in the subsequent reactions.

outcome.observation: After TMS protection of the tertiary alcohol, the diastereoisomers 9a and 9b could be separated by flash column chromatography on silica gel and used individually in the subsequent reactions.

evidence.text_span: Aer TMS protection of the tertiary alcohol, the diastereoisomers 9a and 9b could be separated by ash column chromatography on silica gel and used individually in the subsequent reactions.

thought.background: With 9a and 9b, the next step is dihydroxylation.
thought.gap: How to achieve efficient dihydroxylation.
thought.decision: The Upjohn dihydroxylation was attempted.

outcome.observation: The Upjohn dihydroxylation was found to proceed slowly with low conversion even after 5 days.

evidence.text_span: The Upjohn dihydroxylation was found to proceed slowly with low conversion even aer 5 days.

thought.background: Upjohn dihydroxylation was inefficient.
thought.gap: How to achieve efficient dihydroxylation.
thought.decision: Ruthenium-catalyzed syn-dihydroxylation was chosen.

outcome.observation: Fortunately, ruthenium-catalyzed syn-dihydroxylation could afford the desired diols 10a and 10b as well as over oxidized hydroxyketones 11a and 11b in moderate yields.

evidence.text_span: Fortunately, ruthenium-catalyzed syn-dihydroxylation18 could afford the desired diols 10a and 10b as well as over oxidized hydroxyketones 11a and 11b in moderate yields.

thought.background: The diols 10a/10b were obtained, but also over-oxidized hydroxyketones 11a/11b. The diols can be further oxidized.
thought.gap: How to oxidize the diols 10a/10b to hydroxyketones.
thought.decision: IBX oxidation was used to convert diols 10a/10b to hydroxyketones 11a/11b.

outcome.observation: The diols 10a/10b could also be oxidized to hydroxyketones using IBX. The structures of 10a (CCDC 2464462) and 11b (CCDC 2464461) were further confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

evidence.text_span: The diols 102/10b could also be oxidized to hydroxyketones using IBX. The structures of 10a (CCDC 2464462) and 11b (CCDC 2464461) were further conrmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

thought.background: With 11a and 11b in hand, the next goal was to transform the benzyl acetal to lactone and dehydrate the C-5 tertiary alcohol to a terminal alkene.
thought.gap: How to achieve the dehydration of the C-5 tertiary alcohol.
thought.decision: Dehydration was attempted under various conditions.

outcome.observation: However, we encountered unexpected difficulty in dehydration under various conditions and suspected that the adjacent carbonyl might be responsible for the failure of dehydration.

evidence.text_span: With 11a and 11b in hand, we tried to accomplish the total synthesis by transforming the benzyl acetal to lactone as well as dehydrating the C-5 tertiary alcohol to a terminal alkene. However, we encountered unexpected difficulty in dehydration under various conditions and suspected that the adjacent carbonyl might be responsible for the failure of dehydration.

thought.background: Dehydration of 11a/11b was unsuccessful. An alternative approach was to perform dihydroxylation from 8 and protect as acetonides 13a/13b, then attempt olefination.
thought.gap: How to achieve olefination from acetonides 13a/13b.
thought.decision: Olefination was tested using Wittig reagent, Peterson conditions (TMSCH2MgCl), Tebbe reagent, and Petasis reagent.

outcome.observation: We also tried to perform the dihydroxylation from 8 and protection as acetonides 13a/13b. Then olefination was tested using the Wittig reagent, Peterson conditions (TMSCH2MgCl), Tebbe reagent, and Petasis reagent, but unfortunately, none of them could deliver the desired olefin. Methyl addition and dehydration did not work either due to decomposition.

evidence.text_span: We also tried to perform the dihydroxylation from 8 and protection as acetonides 13a/13b. Then olenation was tested using the Wittig reagent, Peterson conditions (TMSCH2MgCl)),19 Tebbe reagent,20 and Petasis reagent,21 but unfortunately, none of them could deliver the desired olen. Methyl addition and dehydration did not work either due to decomposition.

thought.background: The Achmatowicz rearrangement-based strategy failed due to unsuccessful olefination of the C5 carbonyl, attributed to steric hindrance and electronic effects.
thought.gap: A new strategy is needed to overcome the olefination problem and efficiently construct the d-lactone.
thought.decision: A new strategy was proposed to install the terminal alkene at an early stage through vinyl metallic reagent addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (III), followed by allylic C-H oxidation. The d-lactone would then be constructed by aldol condensation of a lactic acid derivative (V) and a β-hydroxyl-α-methylene-aldehyde (VI) followed by lactonization.

evidence.text_span: This failure forced us to re-design a new synthetic strategy that was not based on the Achmatowicz rearrangement. We recognized that there was one major obstacle in the Achmatowicz rearrangement-based strategy: unsuccessful ole-nation of the C5 carbonyl due to the extraordinary steric hindrance and electronic effects of the neighboring functional groups. In order to avoid this problem, we proposed to install this terminal alkene at an early stage through vinyl metallic reagent addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (Scheme 3a, III + VII → VI]). The d-lactone could be constructed by aldol condensation of lactic acid derivative V and β-hydroxyl-α-methylene-aldehyde VI followed by lactonization.

thought.background: The new strategy involves early installation of the terminal alkene via vinyl metallic reagent addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (5).
thought.gap: How to efficiently add the vinyl metallic reagent to the ketone.
thought.decision: 2-bromoallyl alcohol and TBS protected 2-bromoallyl alcohol were employed for lithium–halogen exchange to generate in situ the requisite alkenyl lithium reagents 15a/15b, which were added to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5.

outcome.observation: Initially, 2-bromoallyl alcohol and TBS protected 2-bromoallyl alcohol were employed for lithium–halogen exchange to generate in situ the requisite alkenyl lithium reagents 15a/15b, which were added to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5 (Scheme 3b). Unfortunately, both reactions gave 16a/16b in poor yields (16% and 25%, respectively). We suspected that the lithium alkoxide might aggregate in the solution and disfavor the nucleophilic addition to the bulky enolizable ketone 5. If tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) ether 15b was used as the protecting group, the propargyl addition product became the major byproduct.

evidence.text_span: Initially, 2-bromoallyl alcohol and TBS protected 2-bromoallyl alcohol were employed for lithium–halogen exchange to generate in situ the requisite alkenyl lithium reagents 15a/15b, which were added to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5 (Scheme 3b). Unfortunately, both reactions gave 16a/16b in poor yields (16% and 25%, respectively). We suspected that the lithium alkoxide might aggregate in the solution and disfavor the nucleophilic addition to the bulky enolizable ketone 5. If tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) ether 15b was used as the protecting group, the propargyl addition product became the major byproduct.

thought.background: Alkenyl lithium reagents gave poor yields in the addition to ketone 5.
thought.gap: How to improve the nucleophilic addition to the bulky enolizable ketone 5.
thought.decision: The isopropenyl Grignard reagent 15c was chosen for the addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5.

outcome.observation: To improve this nucleophilic addition, we chose the isopropenyl Grignard reagent 15c for the addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5.

evidence.text_span: To improve this nucleophilic addition, we chose the isopropenyl Grignard reagent 15c for the addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5 and performed an allylic oxidation with Ph2Se2/PhIO2 (ref. 22) to obtain the aldehyde 17 in 76% yield.

thought.background: After the Grignard addition, the next step is allylic oxidation to form the aldehyde.
thought.gap: How to efficiently perform the allylic oxidation.
thought.decision: Allylic oxidation with SeO2 under conventional conditions was attempted.

outcome.observation: It is noteworthy that the allylic oxidation with SeO2 under conventional conditions proceeded in only 15% yield.

evidence.text_span: It is noteworthy that the allylic oxidation with SeO2 under conventional conditions23 proceeded in only 15% yield.

thought.background: Allylic oxidation with SeO2 gave low yield.
thought.gap: How to improve the yield of allylic oxidation.
thought.decision: Allylic oxidation with Ph2Se2/PhIO2 was chosen.

outcome.observation: We chose the isopropenyl Grignard reagent 15c for the addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5 and performed an allylic oxidation with Ph2Se2/PhIO2 to obtain the aldehyde 17 in 76% yield.

evidence.text_span: To improve this nucleophilic addition, we chose the isopropenyl Grignard reagent 15c for the addition to cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone 5 and performed an allylic oxidation with Ph2Se2/PhIO2 (ref. 22) to obtain the aldehyde 17 in 76% yield.

thought.background: With racemic aldehyde 17 in hand, the next step is to form the d-lactone via aldol condensation and lactonization.
thought.gap: How to achieve the aldol condensation and concomitant lactonization to form the desired lactones.
thought.decision: Aldol condensation of racemic aldehyde 17 and Seebach–Fráter chiral 1,3-dioxolan-4-one 18 was performed, which was expected to lead to intramolecular lactonization.

outcome.observation: Next, aldol condensation of racemic aldehyde 17 and Seebach–Fráter chiral 1,3-dioxolan-4-one 18 (prepared through condensation of (S)-lactic acid and 2,2-dimethylpropanal) afforded an inseparable 1:1 mixture of lactones 20a and 20b, which might be derived from an intramolecular lactonization (19a/b → 20a/b).

evidence.text_span: Next, aldol condensation of racemic aldehyde 17 and Seebach–Fr´ater chiral 1,3-dioxolan-4-one 18 (prepared through condensation of (S)-lactic acid and 2,2-dimethylpropanal)24 afforded an inseparable 1 : 1 mixture of lactones 20a and 20b, which might be derived from an intramolecular lactonization (19a/b → 20a/b).

thought.background: A mixture of lactones 20a and 20b was obtained. The final step is oxidation to yield aogacillin B and ent-aogacillin A.
thought.gap: How to oxidize the lactones 20a/20b to the final products.
thought.decision: IBX oxidation was used to furnish a mixture of aogacillin B and the enantiomer of aogacillin A.

outcome.observation: The subsequent IBX oxidation of 20a/20b furnished a mixture of aogacillin B and the enantiomer of aogacillin A, which were finally separated by reversed-phase preparative HPLC. The spectroscopic data (1H NMR, 13C NMR, and HRMS) and specific rotation of our synthetic materials were consistent with those reported for the natural products (Tables S1 and S2).

evidence.text_span: The subsequent IBX oxidation of 20a/20b furnished a mixture of aogacillin B and the enantiomer of aogacillin A, which were nally separated by reversed-phase preparative HPLC. The spectroscopic data 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and HRMS) and specic rotation of our synthetic materials were consistent with those reported for the natural products (Tables S1 and S2).

thought.background: A mixture of aogacillin B and ent-aogacillin A was obtained from racemic cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (±)-5, requiring HPLC separation.
thought.gap: The HPLC separation was inefficient, leading to low recovery of pure products.
thought.decision: The current approach of separating the final mixture was deemed inefficient.

outcome.observation: It should be noted, however, that the HPLC separation was very inefficient. Only 5.8 mg of pure ent-aogacillin A and 4 mg of pure aogacillin B could be obtained from 40 mg of their mixture.

evidence.text_span: It should be noted, however, that the HPLC separation was very inefficient. Only 5.8 mg of pure ent-aogacillin A and 4 mg of pure aogacillin B could be obtained from 40 mg of their mixture.

thought.background: The racemic starting material (±)-5 led to a mixture of final products that was difficult to separate.
thought.gap: How to achieve an enantioselective synthesis to avoid the inefficient HPLC separation.
thought.decision: To synthesize optically active (or pure) cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (5) using (S)-carvone as the chiral non-racemic starting material.

evidence.text_span: Since a mixture of aogacillin B and ent-aogacillin A was obtained due to the use of racemic cis-2-ethyl-6-methylcyclohexanone (±)-5 and HPLC separation was required, we proposed to synthesize optically active (or pure) cis-2-ethyl-6- methyl-cyclohexanone 5 and expected to achieve the asymmetric synthesis of either aogacillin A or aogacillin B. To this end, we employed (S)-carvone as the chiral non-racemic starting material for the preparation of (+)-5 (Scheme 3d).

thought.background: To synthesize optically active cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (5), (S)-carvone was chosen as the starting material.
thought.gap: How to convert (S)-carvone to the desired cyclohexanone.
thought.decision: α-Alkylation of (S)-carvone with ethyl triflate was performed to afford trans ethyl addition product (-)-22.

outcome.observation: α-Alkylation of (S)-carvone with ethyl triflate afforded the trans ethyl addition product (-)-22 with excellent diastereoselectivity. Although the conversion was low even using 5 equivalents of ethyl triflate, (S)-carvone could be recycled.

evidence.text_span: α-Alkylation of (S)-carvone with ethyl triate afforded the trans ethyl addition product (-)-22 with excellent diastereoselectivity. Although the conversion was low even using 5 equivalents of ethyl triate, (S)- carvone could be recycled.

thought.background: With (-)-22, the next step is conjugate reduction.
thought.gap: How to achieve conjugate reduction with high diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: Conjugate reduction with L-selectride was performed.

outcome.observation: The conjugate reduction with L-selectride could give (-)-23 in 67% yield with high diastereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: The conjugate reduction with L-selectride could give (-)-23 in 67% yield with high diastereoselctivity.

thought.background: With (-)-23, the final step to obtain enantiopure (+)-5 is hydrodealkenylation.
thought.gap: How to achieve enantio-pure cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (+)-5.
thought.decision: Kwon's hydrodealkenylation conditions were used.

outcome.observation: Under Kwon's hydrodealkenylation conditions, enantio-pure cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (+)-5 could be prepared smoothly in 74% yield.

evidence.text_span: Under Kwon's hydrodealkenylation conditions,26 enantio-pure cis-2-ethyl-6-methyl-cyclohexanone (+)-5 could be prepared smoothly in 74% yield.

thought.background: With enantiopure (+)-5, the next steps are Grignard addition and allylic oxidation to obtain enantiopure 17.
thought.gap: How to perform these steps with the enantiopure starting material.
thought.decision: The same Grignard addition and allylic oxidation procedures as for the racemic substrate were followed.

outcome.observation: Following the Grignard addition and allylic oxidation procedures as for the racemic substrate, enantiopure 17 was synthesized.

evidence.text_span: Following the Grignard addition and allylic oxidation procedures as for the racemic substrate, enantiopure 17 was synthesized.

thought.background: With enantiopure 17, the next step is aldol condensation with Seebach–Fráter chiral 1,3-dioxolan-4-one 18.
thought.gap: How to achieve enantiopure lactone 20a.
thought.decision: Aldol condensation with Seebach–Fráter chiral 1,3-dioxolan-4-one 18 was performed.

outcome.observation: The next aldol condensation with Seebach–Fráter chiral 1,3-dioxolan-4-one 18 afforded enantiopure lactone 20a. The absolute configuration of the C-4 hydroxyl group was confirmed by the NOESY spectrum. This was consistent with the DFT calculation results.

evidence.text_span: The next aldol condensation with Seebach–Fr´ater chiral 1,3-dioxolan-4-one 18 afforded enantiopure lactone 20a. The absolute conguration of the C-4 hydroxyl group was conrmed by the NOESY spectrum. This was consistent with the DFT calculation results.

thought.background: With enantiopure lactone 20a, the final step is oxidation to yield ent-aogacillin A.
thought.gap: How to oxidize lactone 20a to ent-aogacillin A.
thought.decision: IBX oxidation was used to accomplish the enantioselective synthesis of ent-aogacillin A.

outcome.observation: Final oxidation with IBX accomplished the enantioselective synthesis of ent-aogacillin A.

evidence.text_span: Final oxidation with IBX accomplished the enantioselective synthesis of ent-aogacillin A.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The total synthesis of ent-aogacillin A and aogacillin B was achieved in 7 steps.

validation.significance: The paper describes the first total synthesis of ent-aogacillin A and aogacillin B. It highlights the challenges associated with synthesizing these densely oxidized spirolactones and introduces a new strategy featuring early installation of the terminal alkene and a novel one-pot aldol reaction/lactonization for d-lactone construction. This new method could be applicable to the synthesis of other functionalized d-lactones and d-lactone-containing natural products.

source.title: Total syntheses of ent-aogacillin A and aogacillin B.
source.authors: Gu H, Li Z, Zhang X, Sang R, Li Z, Ren J, Chen X, Ma B, Qiu W, Yang Z, Li X, Tong R, Zhang W.
